to the text how so well he reads the bible not just as a history book and not just as a book of
right he reads it as a work of art so he looks at the bible as literature like how you would read
shakespeare precisely he looks at it as an absolute master class and storytelling wow
he believes that the ancient hebrew writers were geniuses at their craft
so he pays attention to the little things exactly he pays attention to the dialogue
yeah like what people say to each other and what they don't say yes the silence is huge
he looks at the descriptions too right because every single word is there for a reason alter
firmly believes that if the narrator stops to tell us about a character's hair which actually

that is fascinating so we are going to use his lens his way of seeing things to look at second
samuel chapter 14. we are going to play detective today i love it we are going to look for all the

chapter starts because we are dropped right into the middle of a very messy situation a royal mess
literally yeah we are in the court of king david in jerusalem but things are not happy
the atmosphere is really heavy based purely on the text we have right here the situation is this
king david has a son named absalom but absalom is not at home no he is away in a place called
gishore so he is in exile right he ran away because of a terrible family tragedy and we are
just on chapter 14 so we won't go into all the complex backstory right we don't need to read the
previous chapters yeah the only important thing to know is that absalom killed his brother and then
he fled it is a massive tragedy but now time has passed and the text tells us something very
specific it says king david's heart is turning toward his son absalom he misses him exactly the
grief for the dead brother has faded a little bit yeah and now there's just this empty space where
he's distracted he is sad but david is the king he can't just pick up the phone and say hey come
home everything is fine right because there is justice to think about he's the supreme judge of
the land exactly if he just invites a known murderer back to the dinner table what does
that say about the law it would look terrible so david is stuck he is completely stuck between his
love as a father and his duty as a king and that is exactly where our third character comes in joab
joab he is the commander of the army he is a tough guy very tough he's a deeply pragmatic man
he looks at the king and he just sees a problem yeah the text says no it was clear to joab that
the king's heart was turning to absalom joab sees that the king is not focused on his job and
a distracted king is dangerous for the whole kingdom exactly joab wants to fix this he wants
to stabilize the throne so he decides he needs to get absalom back right so this is our mission for
today's deep dive we want to understand how joab's heart is turning toward his son joab's heart is
trying to fix this broken family how does reconciliation actually happen does it happen
through a direct honest conversation in this case absolutely not it happens through a trick it
happens through a play okay let's dive right into segment one the setup joab and the actress i love
this part of the chapter joab basically becomes a movie director so joab wants david to bring
absalom home but he doesn't just walk into the throne room and tell him that no he doesn't say
hey david stop moping bring your son home
bring your son back why not he's the top general well kings are powerful figures especially a king
like david they can be volatile yeah unpredictable if you tell a king he's being stubborn or if you
try to give him an order you might lose your job might lose your head right joab knows he needs to
be very subtle he needs to be careful he needs to trick the king into seeing the truth on his own
so he has to bypass david's defenses exactly so he sends a message but he doesn't send it to a
lawyer or a royal priest
no he sends a message out to a town called takoa yes takoa why takoa is that like the hollywood of
ancient israel not quite takoa was a small village south of jerusalem okay it was right on the edge
of the wilderness it was rugged so kind of a rural area yes but it was known for its wise people
so by picking a woman from takoa joab is choosing someone who seems authentic she isn't a polished
city person right she isn't some courtier wearing expensive silk she looks like a woman who's
a real everyday person from the countryside and joab wants the king to believe she is just a normal
woman with a normal problem exactly and when she arrives joab gives her a role to play he gives her
very specific instructions on how to look he tells her pretend to be a mourner for our english
learners a mourner is someone who is sad because someone has died right and look at the details
joab gives her here he is very specific about the costume he is styling her he is he is styling her
he tells her put on the clothing of sorrow that means rough simple clothes like sackcloth maybe yeah
uncomfortable clothes and then he says something really interesting he says do not put oil on your
skin let's unpack that because today if you don't put on lotion or oil you just have dry skin right
it's not a big deal but in the ancient world in that hot middle eastern climate oil was life it
really was olive oil was your cosmetic it was your medicine it was a sign of joy exactly if you were
your face shown with oil it meant you were taking care of yourself so by not using oil she would look
dry she would look dusty neglected yes it would look like she'd been crying for weeks and just
hadn't taken care of her body at all no ab wants her to look exhausted he wants david to literally
smell the grief on her he is setting a whole scene he is and this connects perfectly to robert
alter's analysis how so alter talks about this concept of fictionalized history sometimes the
biblical text tells us a story that tells us a story that tells us a story that tells us a story
about a person telling a story ah so it is a story within a story yes joab is the director and this
wise woman is his lead actress but there is one more detail here joab doesn't just give her the
costume no the text says joab put the words in her mouth he wrote the script he completely wrote
the script this is a crucial detail every word she is about to say was designed by joab which
tells us that joab is a major manipulator he treats people like chess pieces he knows exactly
which buttons to push to get a specific reaction from the king it is really interesting that he
uses a woman for this trick though why not send a soldier i mean joab is a general why not send one
of his loyal captains think about what a soldier represents a soldier represents strength and logic
right if a soldier comes in david might put his guard up he might start thinking about military
strategy but a grieving widow that represents raw emotion that represents vulnerability so joab knows
audience he knows king david perfectly yeah he knows david has a soft heart david is a poet after
all exactly david responds to feelings a widow falling on the floor begging for help that is
going to bypass david's logical brain completely go straight to his heart straight to his heart that
makes total sense so she has her costume she has her script she looks the part she is ready let's
move to segment two the parable of the two brothers the performance begins she walks into the king's
house and the text says she falls on her face on the ground and says give me help oh king it is a
very dramatic entrance and david naturally asks what is your trouble right now she starts telling
the story and remember listener this is a lie this is completely made up this is the script
joab wrote yeah but she tells it like it is the absolute truth she is very convincing she says
i'm a widow my husband is dead step one of the script established pity yeah she is
alone in the world in that ancient society a widow was incredibly vulnerable she had no man
to protect her legally or financially then she says i had two sons step two introduced the family
dynamic she says the two sons were out in a field together they got into a fight right and
here is the really tragic part she emphasizes there was no one there to stop them no one to
come between them that detail feels important it is very important she is saying this wasn't a
planned murder it was a sudden fight it was a crime of passion it was a sudden fight it was a crime of passion
so if someone had been there it wouldn't have happened right she is actively softening the
crime and then one son strikes the other and kills him fratricide a brother killing a brother wait
a minute that sounds very familiar it should this is exactly what happened in david's own family
because absalom killed his brother exactly but she doesn't use any names she keeps it completely
anonymous yes she tells a story that perfectly mirrors david's life but the mirror is just a
bit foggy so he doesn't recognize himself in it yet that is the genius of joab's trap yeah she
is getting the king to judge a case that is identical to his own without him realizing it
so in her story one son is dead and one son is alive right but the tragedy gets worse she
says the whole family her clan all her relatives have turned against her the community is angry
they're demanding justice for the dead brother they say to her hand over the one who killed
his brother so that we may put him to death they want the death penalty
and technically under the law they are right they are completely right the law says a murderer must
die they want an eye for an eye but the woman argues that this kind of strict justice will
destroy her completely yes she lost her husband she lost one son if they kill the second son
she has nobody left she will be completely alone and here she uses a very powerful image
this is one of the most beautiful and famous lines in this entire chapter it is
she says they will put out my last burning coal my last burning coal let's really unpack this
metaphor for everyone yes because robert alter highlights this specifically he does what does
a burning coal mean to an ancient listener well imagine a world without matches or lighters right
fire was difficult to make it was incredibly precious in every home you had a hearth or a
fireplace for cooking and warmth right and at night you wouldn't let the fire go out completely
that would be a disaster so what would you do
you would take ashes and cover the fire keeping just one coal glowing red underneath you would
keep it alive yes you preserve that one spark and in the morning you would take that single coal
blow on it add some dry grass and start the fire again for the new day wow that coal was literally
the spark of life for the household so she is saying the surviving sun is that coal he is the
last spark he represents hope he represents the future yeah but even more than that in this culture
he represents the name the family name yes in the biblical world your immortality was tied to
your children right if your name was remembered and carried on you were still alive in a way
if this final son dies the husband's name is erased from history completely it is blotted out
exactly so she is saying to david if you give these relatives what they want if you give them
strict justice you are pouring water on my last burning coal you're extinguishing my family forever
she is the clan calls it justice but she calls it destruction she is making david feel the sheer
horror of wiping out a family line and that is so emotional it makes you feel her desperation yeah
because she isn't arguing about the law at all no she isn't saying my son is innocent
she fully admits he killed his brother she is just arguing for survival correct she is
making a pure emotional appeal she is designing this fiery image to make david feel intense pity
wants him to prioritize life over the law and because david is a father who is currently
missing his own exiled son this hits him incredibly hard it hits him right where he
is most vulnerable right in the heart which leads us perfectly into segment three the trap
from fiction to reality david hears this story about the widow and the coal
and he reacts immediately he falls for it completely hook line and sinker he gives
a royal ruling he tells her go to your house i will give orders about this
he is basically saying i will handle it i will protect your son from the clan but the woman
isn't satisfied with just that she is very clever very she needs to make sure the trap is perfectly
tight so she pushes him she does she basically asks are you sure i don't want the guilt to be
on me if something bad happens she pushes him to make a formal vow and david swears by the lord he
says as the lord lives not a hair of your son's head will come to the earth wow not a hair of your
son's head that means total protection absolute total protection he has made a binding oath before
god yeah he has judged the case and he has decided that saving the burning coal is more important
than punishing the murder and snap the trap shuts he has set a legal precedent now comes
the pivot the woman asks to say one more thing to the king this is the moment where the music would
stop in the movie yes she completely changes her tone she drops the act she says why have you had
thought about the people of god for in saying these very words the king has put himself in the
wrong could you just imagine the shock in that room it must have been completely silent this
weeping dusty widow suddenly stands up straight she stops crying she looks the king right in the
eye and basically says you are the hypocrite she says you have a banished one absalom and you
haven't brought him home she directly points out his hypocrisy she shows him that he is willing to
save his own son who is a murderer but he won't save his own real son who is also a murderer she
used his own judgment against him it is a brilliant legal trap but she doesn't stop there
no she needs to convince him to actually take action and bring absalom back so she gives us
another incredible metaphor she stops talking about coal and fire and she starts talking about
water yes she says we are like water drained out on the earth which it is not possible to take up
again this is a really profound observation on the fragility of life it is water drained out on the
earth i just picture taking a cup of water and throwing it onto the dry desert ground right it
disappears instantly you can't ever gather it back up exactly you cannot put the water back
in the cut she is talking about death here specifically the death of amnon the brother who
was killed yes she is saying amnon is dead he is gone just like spilled water she is telling david
that absalom won't bring amnon back keeping absalom in exile won't fix the past either the
past is spilled water it is just gone you need to focus on the future the future is the burning coal
the past is the spilled water that is a beautiful way to put it don't weep over the spilled water
protect the burning coal it is a deeply pragmatic argument very practical it is but then she switches
to theology she does she makes an argument about god she says god will not take away the life of
the man whose purpose
is that he who has been sent away may not be completely cut off from him that sentence
is a bit complex in english let's break that down for our learners please do what does she actually
mean she's saying that god devises means that is a key phrase god makes plans god invents ways
to fix things okay she's arguing that god is fundamentally a god of restoration so god doesn't
just want to punish people no she says that when someone is banished or lost god tries to find a way
to bring them back so she is telling david you should act like god exactly god finds a way to
fix broken things you need to find a way to bring your son home she is directly appealing to david's
faith she is saying be merciful just like god is merciful precisely and notice the language she
uses here robert alter points this out beautifully what does he say he calls this high rhetoric high
rhetoric that means fancy beautiful complex speech right she isn't talking like a simple farm woman
from tucoa anymore no she is speaking like a philosopher or a prophet why does she do that
does she just want to impress him i think she wants to confuse and convince him at the same time the
beauty of the language acts almost like a spell yeah it makes the argument sound incredibly true
because it sounds so beautiful water drained on the earth it is poetry and poetry is powerful it
is and it works on david he listens but david is a smart guy isn't he very smart he has been
a king for a long time he has to realize what is actually going on eventually he absolutely does
the spell breaks and that brings us right to segment four the discovery david looks at this

like an angel of god knowing all things that are on the earth she is trying to save herself yeah

nothing can be hidden from you it is a really smart move it keeps david from getting angry at

it tells us two important things okay first it tells us about his intense vanity absalom is a
proud man he grows his hair long to show off his vitality his strength it is a symbol of his power
and second it tells us about his value to the regular people
people 200 shekels is literally a weight of silver so his hair is quite literally worth
money he is the golden boy the people love him because he is beautiful and striking he looks

text focuses this heavily on someone's physical beauty it is almost always a warning a warning yes
it signals that they might be shallower or that their outer beauty
covers up something dark on the inside or that their beauty will literally be their downfall
spoiler alert for the rest of the larger story his hair does play a role in his death later on
